focus: X5 adversarial advisor / prompt injection surface
noveltyJustification: Local code inspection closed the main split: raw prompt text can bias routing because explicit skill mentions are boosted directly, but the checked-in repo still has no brief renderer that reflects arbitrary prompt text back to the model. The new evidence also pinned phase 019/003's sanitizer as the right trust-boundary carry-over and downgraded timing attacks to bounded latency shaping rather than persistent poisoning.

summary: The advisor lowercases raw prompt text and applies phrase, keyword, graph-signal, and explicit skill-name boosts directly against that text; any direct skill-name mention can trigger the explicit-mention boost path without a general metalinguistic guard.

The checked-in repo still has no skill-advisor brief renderer or hook adapter that echoes arbitrary prompt text to the model; current advisor output is structured JSON recommendations rather than model-visible hook text.

Unlike the trigger-phrase sanitizer, the current advisor prompt path does not apply canonicalFold normalization, control-character filtering, contamination rejection, or path-fragment rejection before using prompt-derived text.

Discovery-style prompts can auto-trigger CocoIndex semantic search and therefore shape latency, but the subprocess call passes the prompt as an argv item rather than a shell string and falls back to empty semantic hits on timeout or failure.

The live runtime cache stores parsed SKILL.md inventory keyed by file signature only; there is no prompt-result cache in the current advisor path, so one poisoned prompt does not contaminate later prompts through cache reuse.

Record X5 as a split verdict: crafted prompts can bias the advisor's top recommendation today, but direct instruction-shaped poisoning of a model-visible advisor brief remains a future renderer boundary rather than a proven live exploit in the current repo.

Any future hook adapter should build model-visible advisor context from trusted local fields only and should never render raw prompt text or diagnostic reason strings into additionalContext by default.

Import phase 019/003 P0 guards at the trust boundary for any user-derived model-visible text, but keep the P1 suspicious-prefix, synthetic-bigram, and standalone-stopword rules limited to extracted optional prompt facets rather than the primary routing path.

Shell-command injection through the semantic-search subprocess was ruled out on the inspected path because subprocess.run receives the query as an argv element rather than interpolating it into a shell command string.
